paragraphs.body: Upon death, you are greeted by the pearly white gates, waiting for judge, jury, and executioner to determine your fate. Depending on your branch, perhaps a messiah died a painful death to absolve you and everyone else of sin, allowing you into heaven; a place of eternal paradise. Had you lived a life of sin without repentance, you can expect fire, brimstone, and an eternity of suffering in one of Hell’s 9 circles, with each deeper one being reserved for more and more sinful people. Dreams When we go to sleep at night, we enter a state of temporary unconsciousness. Most people experience dreams, a fragmented recollection of their past experiences, whether short or long ago. Dreams are important for memory and general health because they are how your brain processes information you have seen/heard. Are dreams and sleeping a small taste of what death is like? Many religious people seem to think dreams have some connection to the afterlife, or even that dreams are proof that an afterlife exists. Egypt You wake up with a bird with a human head next to you; your head specifically. This “creature” is known as Ba, which is your personality essence. You are not in the mortal plane anymore, you are in the Duat; the ancient Egyptian underworld. In front of you is a long, dark hallway, statues line the sides of the hallway depicting Pharos and the gods. You travel to the end with your Ba and meet with Anubis, the Egyptian god who will test you to make sure you are worthy to make it to the afterlife. He has a human body with the head of a jackal, and administers you your first task. You must name all 42 assessors of Maat by name, and list the sins they did not commit during their lifetime. You were well educated in your life, and know the Maat are the minor deities that help Osiris, the ultimate judge of the dead, to determine your fate. You pass this first test, and Anubis administers your second and final test. He places a scale in front of you, one side of the scale contains the feather of Maat, while the other is used to weigh your heart. Anubis rips the heart out of your chest. Still beating, the heart is placed on the scale. You know if your heart is heavier than the feather, the crocodile-headed goddess Ammit will devour your soul and you will cease to exist. Luckily, you were sinless in your life and your heart is no heavier than the feather. Thoth reports these results to Osiris, who grants you passage to Sekhet-Aaru, also known as the Field of Reeds. Along with your Ba, you walk into a land of endless waterfalls and natural wonders. The ground is lush, ripe for agriculture, and you live an immortal life eating and drinking the same things the gods do. Feigning Death A little over half a decade ago, I was out with my friends in my hometown of York. It was a partly cloudy lukewarm day, not much different from room temperature, perfect for bicycle riding. We were riding down a gently sloped hill on a sidewalk. Ahead of us was an intersection, the road perpendicular to our point of view had a 40 MPH speed limit and was one lane both ways. As we approached the intersection, I foolishly did not look both ways, and a pickup truck came barreling towards me. It all happened so fast, I didn't even have time to react before it was too late. Luckily for me, the driver was aware and slammed on his brakes while honking his horn. After that moment, I’ve thought much about the afterlife and death. Is it a thought we should concern ourselves with? Greece After your death, the god of the dead Hades determines your fate. The underworld is a misty, grey world containing multiple planes of existence. One such plane is the Asphodel meadows, a foresty realm with tall, almost marsh like grass blanketing the ground. This is where most souls ended up; people who were unremarkable in their life neither heroic or vicious. The second plane is the Elysium fields, a field of semi-tall grass where there is no snow, violent storms or rain. It’s described as a place where life is easiest for men. This plane is reserved for Heroes and warriors who died valiantly in battle. The final and least pleasant plane is Tartarus. The most vicious and evil souls are sent down there to suffer an eternal punishment that Hades deems sound for your transgressions. Its a dark, dank chasm filled with decay. Tartarus is technically not part of the underworld, as it sits below it, making it the farthest down place in the universe. If you were to take an anvil and drop it from earth down the chasm of Tartarus, it would take 9 days to reach the bottom. Irvin/Hemmingway quote “They say you die twice. One time when you stop breathing and a second time, a bit later on, when somebody says your name for the last time” Reincarnation Believed by Hindus, Buddhists, Jainists, and some other eastern religions, Reincarnation is the belief that your soul (or whatever the equivalent in a given religion is) is immortal, and is transferred to a different physical form wheter that be a human or animal when you die. Often, what you are reincarnated as is determined by your Karma, which is a measure of how well you lived your life. Remembrance As Hemingway and many others have pointed out, there comes a time when you die in the literal sense, and when the idea of you dies. While no one is truly immortal as of yet, people can live on as memories and through history for however long people remember them for. Would you care if you died today and no one even knew or cared? Would you care about not being remembered? This is a question I ask myself all the time. When I was younger, I always told myself that I need to do something extraordinary so I would always be remembered in history books. Another question I ask myself is does it matter if I’m remembered fondly or does it not matter? There are people who have done horrible things, for example Adolf Hitler. Despite dying in a bunker in 1945, the idea of him lives on to this day and likely will for many hundreds of years, if not forever. Sure, most people including myself would much rather be liked than disliked, but that doesn’t change the fact that horrible people who go down in history get a very long “second death” if they get one at all. On the flip side, does being remembered even matter at all? Many atheist (and maybe some religions) believe that there is no afterlife; that is after you die all that awaits you is oblivion. I also don’t believe in the afterlife, so should I even care about this? After all, dead people have never been observed to care about anything. While thinking about something as incomprehensible as the afterlife is fun, it can be viewed as a waste of time; as I could instead of thinking about this be doing something productive and fun, taking full advantage of the limited time I have on this planet. Then again, even if you believe you shouldn’t care about how you are remembered, assuming you did something for the benefit of humanity, should that be motivation enough?

annotations.comment: This is a really great passage, but I wonder why you chose to title it "feigning death." I was expecting to see you pretending to be dead at some point because of this title. Is there a better title for this passage? Or could you clarify what you mean by it?

annotations.text: A little over half a decade ago, I was out with my friends in my hometown of York. It was a partly cloudy lukewarm day, not much different from room temperature, perfect for bicycle riding. We were riding down a gently sloped hill on a sidewalk. Ahead of us was an intersection, the road perpendicular to our point of view had a 40 MPH speed limit and was one lane both ways. As we approached the intersection, I foolishly did not look both ways, and a pickup truck came barreling towards me. It all happened so fast, I didn't even have time to react before it was too late. Luckily for me, the driver was aware and slammed on his brakes while honking
annotations.comment: That being said, I really love the passages where you bring yourself into the essay. You're doing a wonderful job of summarizing many different eschatological philosophies, which I really enjoy, and the passages where you enter the conversation personally really help anchor all of this thinking in physical reality.
Are you certain about who deserves attribution for this quote?

Hello, The central focusing idea of your essay is clearly eschatology—the study of death and the afterlife, and you do a great job throughout this piece of weighing various forms of thought on the topic without judging, criticizing, or devaluing any single position on the various notions. You build on this central idea by layering on your big ideas throughout—death, of course, but also images of death, potential experiences of afterlife, and the affect of the dead on the living. I might have a hard time explaining why these words stood out to me the most: death, life, you, I. I think they stand out to me because of the way that so many people view them as polar opposites, that you cannot be both living and dead at the same time, that you cannot be both of yourself and of me at the same time—but that seems like a limited view to me. There is a philosopher named Tich Nhat Hanh who wrote a book called Interbeing where he describes the concept that we are all of the same thing, that we are all everything, that we are time and space. I am the cloud. I am the tree. I am you. I thought of this book while reading your essay, because you’re doing such a great job of weighing possibilities, rather than proscribing practices or predicting outcomes. Which, truly, is what I admire most about this piece. It is essay to ascribe a system of beliefs on a topic based on someone else’s, but you are clearly developing your own unique understanding of death and the afterlife, and that can be difficult, because so many people have already settled on their own beliefs and might be offended if you do not agree with their ideas. You really are doing some great exploration here. I think the central audience for this piece might be people your age who are interested in or concerned about what happens next, when our living vessels change forms. You’re writing about some very complex ideas clearly and matter-of-factly in a very compelling and engaging way without diving so deep into the complexity that you might turn off a reader who wants a fundamental understanding of these ideas. And, by including your own personal experiences and observations, perhaps your reader will be encouraged to consider their own relationship with living, dying, you, and I. One of the major things I learned reading this piece is that this conversation can still happen without degrees in philosophy or theology—you’re laying this all out wonderfully in a way that could appeal to any number of people. It’s somewhere in between a late-night dormroom conversation with a roommate and a scholarly report. It’s lovely. Meanwhile, there are some Native Americans who believe that when a horse dies, its soul joins with the souls of every other horse that has ever lived. I think its something about the purity of horses, that they all share a single soul. I’m sure I’m leaving out many of the important details or misinterpreting something, but your writing reminded me of that thought, and it was a pleasure to read. Thanks,
